draft.introduction: The final chapter of the Bhagavad Gita builds to a crescendo. After seventeen chapters of teaching on knowledge, action, devotion, and the nature of reality, Krishna delivers his ultimate instruction in verses 18.65–66. The teaching is breathtakingly simple after so much complexity: fix your mind on me, be devoted to me, sacrifice to me, bow to me. Abandon all other dharmas and take refuge in me alone. I shall liberate you from all sins. Do not grieve. These verses are the foundation of the bhakti (devotion) tradition in Hinduism. Where the Upanishads teach liberation through knowledge (jñāna) and the earlier chapters of the Gita teach liberation through desireless action (karma), here Krishna offers a third path: total surrender to the divine person. This is theosis not as philosophical realization but as love—the complete giving of oneself to God, and God’s complete acceptance in return. Ramanuja built his entire theology on these two verses.

translation.text: Fix your mind on me, be devoted to me, sacrifice to me, bow down to me. You shall come to me. Truly I promise you: you are dear to me.
translation.original_text: manmanā bhava madbhakto madyājī māṃ namaskuru | mām evaiṣyasi satyaṃ te pratijāne priyo’si me

translation.text: Abandoning all dharmas (duties), take refuge in me alone. I shall liberate you from all sins. Do not grieve.
translation.original_text: sarvadharman parityajya mām ekaṃ śaraṇaṃ vraja | ahaṃ tvā sarvapāpebhyo mokṣayiṣyāmi mā śucaḥ

translator_notes.note: The phrase priyo’si me (“you are dear to me”) transforms the entire register of the Gita. For seventeen chapters, Krishna has been a teacher—authoritative, cosmic, sometimes terrifying (as in the vision of chapter 11). Here he speaks as a lover. The divine does not merely tolerate or instruct the human being; it loves the human being. This personal, emotional quality of the divine-human relationship is what distinguishes bhakti from jñāna (knowledge) and karma (action) as paths to liberation.

The instruction sarvadharman parityajya (“abandoning all dharmas”) is the most radical statement in the Gita. Dharma—duty, law, righteousness—is the foundation of Hindu ethics and social order. The entire Gita has been about dharma: what it is, why it matters, how to fulfil it. And now, at the very end, Krishna says: abandon it all. Take refuge in me alone. This parallels the Christian teaching that the law is fulfilled and transcended by grace (Romans 10:4, Galatians 3:24–25). The Gita does not reject dharma; it subordinates it to something higher: the direct relationship between the soul and God.
